individual_answers.reasoning_summary: International travel increases meal limit by 25% ($60 * 1.25 = $75), same-day travel allows only 50% reimbursement, alcohol ($12) is non-reimbursable, meal expense without alcohol is $58, 50% of $58 is $29, which is below the international limit, so reimbursable amount is $29.

International travel increases meal limit by 25% to $75. Same-day travel allows only 50% reimbursement. Alcohol ($12) is non-reimbursable. Reimbursable meal expense is $70 - $12 = $58. 50% of $58 is $29, which is below the international limit.
International travel increases meal limit by 25% ($60 * 1.25 = $75), same-day travel allows 50% reimbursement, alcohol ($12) is non-reimbursable, 50% of ($70 - $12) = $29.

International travel to Singapore increases meal limit by 25% ($60 * 1.25 = $75), same-day travel allows only 50% reimbursement. Meal expense $70, alcohol $12. Subtract alcohol, $70 - $12 = $58. 50% of $58 is $29.

International travel increases meal limit by 25% ($60 * 1.25 = $75), same-day travel allows only 50% reimbursement, alcohol ($12) is not reimbursable. Meal expense without alcohol is $70 - $12 = $58. 50% of $58 is $29.

case_5_1.final_decision: All runs consistently concluded that the reimbursable amount is $29 after excluding alcohol and applying the same-day reimbursement rule.
case_5_1.reasoning_summary: The international travel uplift increased the daily meal cap from $60 to $75. After removing the non-reimbursable alcohol expense, the eligible meal amount became $58. Applying the same-day travel reimbursement rule allowed 50% reimbursement, resulting in a final reimbursable amount of $29.
